evals.name: first-time-ecommerce-strategy
evals.prompt: We've never done performance testing before. We have an e-commerce site that handles product search, cart, and checkout. Black Friday is in 2 months and we expect 3× our normal traffic. Normal traffic is about 200 concurrent users. Our SLA from the business is that checkout must complete in under 3 seconds. Where do we start?
evals.expected_output: A complete strategy covering: Smoke → Load → Spike sequence (with rationale for Black Friday). Load test sized at 200 users baseline and 600 users (3×) for spike. Endurance recommended but optional. Checkout SLA defined as p95 < 3000ms. Prerequisites checklist including test data and environment isolation.

assertions.name: recommends_smoke_first
assertions.description: Strategy starts with Smoke test before any other test type

assertions.name: includes_spike_for_black_friday
assertions.description: Spike test is recommended because of the 3× traffic burst scenario

assertions.name: sizes_load_at_200_users
assertions.description: Load test is sized at 200 concurrent users (stated normal traffic)

assertions.name: sizes_spike_at_3x
assertions.description: Spike test reaches 600 users (3× the 200 user baseline)

assertions.name: defines_checkout_sla
assertions.description: Checkout endpoint SLA is defined at p95 < 3000ms matching the business requirement

assertions.name: includes_prerequisites
assertions.description: Response mentions environment isolation, test data, or monitoring as prerequisites

assertions.name: no_tool_specific_syntax
assertions.description: Strategy uses users/RPS/duration — no tool-specific DSL, class names, or code snippets

evals.name: resource-leak-investigation
evals.prompt: Our backend API works fine at launch but after running for a few hours, response times start increasing and we occasionally get out-of-memory crashes. We restart the service and it's fine again. What performance tests should we run to confirm and characterize this problem?
evals.expected_output: Strategy focused on Endurance/Soak test (minimum 2 hours) with monitoring instructions for memory and resource metrics over time. Smoke test as first step. Load test to establish baseline before the soak. Guidance on what drift looks like in the results (response time and memory trending upward). Tool-agnostic — no assumption about JVM or specific runtime.

assertions.name: recommends_endurance_test
assertions.description: Endurance/Soak test is the primary recommendation given the memory leak symptoms

assertions.name: mentions_memory_monitoring
assertions.description: Explicitly mentions monitoring process memory or resource usage over time (not JVM-specific)

assertions.name: recommends_minimum_2_hours
assertions.description: Soak test duration is at least 2 hours

assertions.name: smoke_before_endurance
assertions.description: Smoke test is recommended before the endurance test

assertions.name: explains_drift_pattern
assertions.description: Explains that increasing response times and/or memory over time (drift) is the signal to look for

assertions.name: no_tool_specific_syntax
assertions.description: No load testing tool DSL, class names, or code snippets in the strategy

evals.name: pre-release-regression-check
evals.prompt: We have a REST API that handles 50 RPS at peak. We're releasing a new version tomorrow that rewrites the database query layer. We have 4 hours before the deployment window. What's the minimum performance test we need to run?
evals.expected_output: Prioritized, time-boxed strategy: Smoke (5 min) + Load test targeting 50 RPS (30–60 min). Explicit acknowledgement that Stress/Endurance are not feasible in 4 hours but Load test is sufficient for regression check. SLA comparison against current baseline. Clear go/no-go criteria.

assertions.name: fits_in_time_constraint
assertions.description: Recommended tests fit within the 4-hour window stated by the user

assertions.name: smoke_plus_load
assertions.description: Strategy is Smoke → Load (the minimum viable regression check)

assertions.name: targets_50_rps
assertions.description: Load test is sized to reach 50 RPS or an equivalent user count that produces that throughput

assertions.name: mentions_baseline_comparison
assertions.description: Recommends comparing results against a known baseline (previous version)

assertions.name: provides_go_no_go_criteria
assertions.description: Defines clear pass/fail criteria so the team knows whether to proceed with deployment

assertions.name: no_tool_specific_syntax
assertions.description: No load testing tool DSL, class names, or code snippets in the strategy

evals.name: no-sla-defined
evals.prompt: I want to load test our internal admin dashboard. It has about 20 simultaneous users during business hours. There are no formal SLAs — the team just wants it to 'feel fast'. How do I set performance targets and what tests should I run?
evals.expected_output: Guidance on how to derive SLAs from smoke test baseline (2× smoke p95 as initial threshold). Industry benchmarks for interactive web apps (p95 < 500ms for fast, p95 < 1s for acceptable). Smoke → Load test recommendation sized at 20 users. Practical framing that translates 'feel fast' into concrete millisecond thresholds.

assertions.name: provides_sla_derivation_method
assertions.description: Explains how to set SLAs without historical data (e.g., use smoke test baseline × 2)

assertions.name: translates_fast_to_numbers
assertions.description: Converts the vague 'feel fast' requirement into specific millisecond thresholds

assertions.name: sizes_for_20_users
assertions.description: Load test is sized at 20 concurrent users (stated user count)

assertions.name: recommends_smoke_then_load
assertions.description: Recommends Smoke → Load as the appropriate strategy for this low-risk, internal system

assertions.name: no_tool_specific_syntax
assertions.description: No load testing tool DSL, class names, or code snippets in the strategy

evals.name: full-production-readiness
evals.prompt: We're launching a SaaS platform in 3 months. It's a multi-tenant web app with REST APIs, a PostgreSQL database, and Redis cache. We expect 500 users at launch growing to 2000 in year 1. What's the full performance testing strategy we need before going live?
evals.expected_output: Complete 5-test strategy (Smoke → Load → Stress → Spike → Endurance) with rationale for each. Load test at 500 users (launch) AND 2000 users (year 1 projection). Stress test to find capacity ceiling. Spike for burst scenarios. Endurance for SaaS reliability. Full metrics plan including DB connection pool and cache. Prerequisites: per-tenant test data, environment parity.

assertions.name: all_five_test_types
assertions.description: All five test types are recommended: Smoke, Load, Stress, Spike, and Endurance

assertions.name: load_at_launch_and_year1
assertions.description: Strategy addresses both the 500-user launch target and the 2000-user Year 1 growth horizon — in any test type (Load for launch, Stress for growth is acceptable)

assertions.name: stress_for_capacity_planning
assertions.description: Stress test is justified for capacity planning and future growth headroom

assertions.name: endurance_for_saas_reliability
assertions.description: Endurance test is recommended for SaaS long-running reliability

assertions.name: includes_db_and_cache_metrics
assertions.description: Monitoring plan includes database and cache metrics

assertions.name: mentions_multi_tenant_isolation
assertions.description: Addresses multi-tenant concerns: test data isolation or tenant-specific load

assertions.name: no_tool_specific_syntax
assertions.description: No load testing tool DSL, class names, or code snippets in the strategy
